design_philosophy.core_loop: Progress through side-scrolling levels combining platforming and run-and-gun combat, acquiring weapon upgrades and character switches, punctuated by vehicle-based gameplay segments and cutscene narrative bookends
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Navigation hazard avoidance combined with combat resource management (weapon ammo/cooldown); difficulty spikes at boss encounters
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Embody Star Wars protagonist(s) across iconic film moments: Luke's journey from farm boy to Jedi, Han Solo's piloting prowess, culminating in the Death Star trench run
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Faithful thematic adaptation that enforces character-specific abilities and story progression; non-negotiable level-by-level narrative flow; vehicle segments break mechanical monotony but are mandatory, not optional; weapon upgrade system gates progression

difficulty.lives_system: Multiple lives with continue system; debug mode allows setting lives up to 99
difficulty.continues_system: Standard continue system present; debug menu accessible via button codes
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based checkpointing within levels; brief cutscenes act as natural breaks between major sections
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Minimal. No difficulty selection mentioned in sources. Debug mode (requires button code cheat) enables invincibility and stage skip, suggesting developer acknowledgment of difficulty but no official adjustable difficulty modes

progression.upgrade_system: Blaster weapon variants available as pickups; lightsaber acquired from Obi-Wan Kenobi; standard blaster as baseline
progression.unlock_mechanism: Character unlock driven by story: Luke Skywalker → Han Solo → Chewbacca; vehicle segments (X-Wing, Landspeeder) unlock at specific story points
replayability_hooks: Multiple playable characters with different abilities encourage replay for varied mechanics
Hidden level select cheat code allows non-linear level access
Weapon variety and positioning depth within fixed levels
Score/performance tracking implicit in arcade-inspired design

world_building_techniques: Brief cutscenes between levels deliver abbreviated film plot
Character-specific dialogue and ability sets reinforce hero identities
Level design directly references film locations: Jawa sandcrawler, Death Star trench run
John Williams orchestral soundtrack (arranged for SNES 8-channel chip) grounds player in Star Wars universe
Enemy and boss roster pulled directly from film canon (Darth Vader as final boss in TIE Advanced)

boss_design.philosophy: Film-iconic encounters reimagined as combat challenges; difficulty spike markers that test accumulated skill and weapon knowledge
notable_bosses: Kalhar Boss Monster (inspired by Millennium Falcon chess piece from film)
Darth Vader (final encounter piloting TIE Advanced x1)

faction_indicators: Imperials vs. Rebels (structural opposition driving level design)
Character selection signals player alignment: Luke = heroic, Han = rogue pragmatist
danger_signals: Blaster fire and enemy placement density increases toward level conclusions
Boss encounters preceded by environmental compression/narrowing
Health attrition mechanics force resource awareness
environmental_storytelling: Jawa sandcrawler interior architecture communicates scrap-salvage setting
Death Star geometry and trench design echo film cinematography
Vehicle segments (landspeeder, X-Wing) contextualize protagonist capabilities within universe physics
Enemy type variety (stormtroopers, creatures) populates Star Wars ecosystem without explicit dialogue

sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor (tcrf.net) - Super Star Wars (SNES)
